en.extract:
The fawn-breasted brilliant (Heliodoxa rubinoides) is a medium-sized species of hummingbird in the family Trochilidae. It is native to South America, where it occurs in Bolivia, Colombia, Ecuador, and Peru. Other common names include lilac-throated brilliant in English and brillante pechigamuza, colibrí de vientre ocre, and diamante pechigamuza in Spanish.


Taxonomy
The fawn-breasted brilliant was originally described as Trochilus rubinoides by French naturalists Jules Bourcier and Étienne Mulsant in 1846, with the type locality given as Colombia. It was later moved to the genus Heliodoxa, which was introduced by John Gould in 1850. The genus name Heliodoxa derives from the Greek words hēlios ("sun") and doxa ("glory"), while the specific epithet rubinoides combines the Latin rubinus ("ruby-red") with the Greek suffix -oidēs ("resembling"), referring to the male's pink throat patch.
There are three recognized subspecies:

Heliodoxa rubinoides rubinoides (Bourcier & Mulsant, 1846) – Central and Eastern Andes of Colombia.
Heliodoxa rubinoides aequatorialis (Gould, 1860) – Western slope of the Andes from western Colombia (Antioquia) south to western Ecuador (Pichincha to El Oro and western Loja).
Heliodoxa rubinoides cervinigularis (Salvin, 1892) – Eastern slope of the Andes in eastern Ecuador and Peru; also recorded in northern Cochabamba, central Bolivia.


Description
The fawn-breasted brilliant is a medium-sized hummingbird, measuring 11 to 13 cm (4.3 to 5.1 in) in length and weighing approximately 7.2 to 10.2 g (0.25 to 0.36 oz). The bill is stout, fairly long (22–27 mm (0.87–1.06 in)), slightly decurved, and black.
Adult males have shining bronzy-green upperparts, with coppery tones on the wing coverts and a slightly forked, coppery-green tail. The underparts are warm buffy-cinnamon, with irregular green spotting on the flanks and throat sides. A distinctive feature is the small, isolated pinkish to lilac gorget patch on the lower throat. Females are similar but have duller plumage, more extensive green spotting on the underparts, and a smaller or absent throat patch. Juveniles resemble females but have buffy fringes on the head feathers.


Distribution and habitat
The fawn-breasted brilliant is found in the Andes from western Colombia south to central Bolivia. It inhabits humid montane forests and cloud forests, primarily in the lower to mid-levels of the forest interior. It occasionally occurs at shaded forest borders, clearings, and near feeders. The species is found at elevations between 1,000 and 2,600 m (3,300 and 8,500 ft), though most records are from 1,100 to 2,200 m (3,600 to 7,200 ft). It is generally uncommon and patchily distributed in natural habitats, but can be more frequently observed at nectar feeders.


Behavior


Diet
The diet of the fawn-breasted brilliant consists primarily of nectar from a variety of brightly colored, tubular flowers. It supplements this with small insects and spiders, which provide essential protein, especially during the breeding season. The bird catches insect prey by hawking in flight, gleaning from foliage, and plucking from spider webs. When feeding on nectar, it hovers before the flower or perches and hangs from it, inserting its long tongue up to 13 times per second. The species is strongly territorial around high-quality nectar sources, aggressively chasing away intruders such as other hummingbirds, bumblebees, and hawk moths.


Breeding
The fawn-breasted brilliant is only social during the breeding season, which spans from August to December in much of its range. The male performs a courtship display by flying in U-shaped patterns before the female. There is no pair bond, and the male is not involved with nesting or the rearing of the young. Both males and females mate with several partners.
The female constructs a compact, cup-shaped nest in a shrub or tree, typically in the forest understory. The nest is made of plant fibers, spider webs, animal hairs, and down, and it is lined externally with moss for camouflage. She lays a clutch of two white eggs, which she incubates alone for approximately 18–20 days. The female broods the chicks for about 12 days, after which there is not enough room for her in the nest. She feeds the nestlings by regurgitating insects, as they cannot persist on nectar alone. The young fledge at about 20 days of age.


Vocalizations

The fawn-breasted brilliant produces two main vocalizations: a series of sharp, metallic "tchik" notes, often given in rapid succession as an alarm or territorial warning, and a common "swi-swi-swi-swu" call. Non-vocal communication includes the audible buzzing of wings during flight and displays, as well as tail fanning during aggressive or courtship interactions.


Conservation
The fawn-breasted brilliant is classified as Least Concern on the IUCN Red List due to its extremely large geographic range, which exceeds 1,100,000 km2 (420,000 sq mi). However, the global population trend is suspected to be decreasing as a result of ongoing habitat loss and fragmentation, primarily from deforestation, agricultural expansion, and mining. The species is listed on Appendix II of CITES, which regulates international trade. It occurs in several protected areas across its range, including Podocarpus National Park in Ecuador.


Gallery


References


Further reading

Ridgely, R. S. and P. J. Greenfield (2001). "Fawn-breasted Brilliant". The Birds of Ecuador: Status, Distribution, and Taxonomy. Volume 1. p. 363.
Ridgely, R. S. and P. J. Greenfield (2001). "Fawn-breasted Brilliant". The Birds of Ecuador: Field Guide. Volume 2. Plate 43, p. 270.

The '''fawn-breasted brilliant''' ('''''Heliodoxa rubinoides''''') is a medium-sized [[species]] of [[hummingbird]] in the family [[Trochilidae]]. It is native to [[South America]], where it occurs in [[Bolivia]], [[Colombia]], [[Ecuador]], and [[Peru]].<ref name=IUCN/><ref name="ebird">{{cite web |title=Fawn-breasted Brilliant |url=https://ebird.org/species/fabbri1 |publisher=eBird |access-date=2026-04-19}}</ref> Other common names include '''lilac-throated brilliant''' in [[English language|English]] and {{lang|es|brillante pechigamuza}}, {{lang|es|colibrí de vientre ocre}}, and {{lang|es|diamante pechigamuza}} in [[Spanish language|Spanish]].<ref name="avian">{{cite web |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211106080255/https://www.beautyofbirds.com/fawnbreastedbrillianthummingbirds.html |title=Fawn-breasted Brilliant Hummingbirds |publisher=Beautyofbirds |access-date=2026-04-19}}</ref><ref name="Avibase">{{cite web |title=Heliodoxa rubinoides |url=https://avibase.bsc-eoc.org/species.jsp?avibaseid=06B7366C5CC58053 |publisher=Avibase |access-date=2026-04-19}}</ref>

==Taxonomy==
The fawn-breasted brilliant was originally described as ''Trochilus rubinoides'' by French naturalists [[Jules Bourcier]] and [[Étienne Mulsant]] in 1846, with the type locality given as Colombia.<ref name="Avibase"/><ref name="Bourcier1846">{{cite journal |last1=Bourcier |first1=Jules |last2=Mulsant |first2=Étienne |title=Description de vingt espèces nouvelles d'oiseaux-mouches |journal=Annales des sciences physiques et naturelles, d'agriculture et d'industrie |volume=9 |year=1846 |pages=312–332 |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/45018930 |language=fr}}</ref> It was later moved to the genus ''[[Heliodoxa]]'', which was introduced by [[John Gould]] in 1850.<ref name="Gould1850">{{cite book |last=Gould |first=John |title=A monograph of the Trochilidæ, or family of humming-birds |volume=4 |year=1860 |publisher=Taylor and Francis |location=London |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/108334#page/274/mode/1up}}</ref> The genus name ''Heliodoxa'' derives from the Greek words ''hēlios'' ("sun") and ''doxa'' ("glory"), while the specific epithet ''rubinoides'' combines the Latin {{Lang|la|rubinus}} ("ruby-red") with the Greek suffix ''-oidēs'' ("resembling"), referring to the male's pink throat patch.<ref name="Jobling">{{cite book |last=Jobling |first=James A. |title=Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names |year=2010 |publisher=Christopher Helm |location=London |isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4 |pages=188, 340}}</ref>

There are three recognized [[subspecies]]:<ref name="ioc">{{cite web |editor1-last=Gill |editor1-first=Frank |editor2-last=Donsker |editor2-first=David |title=Hummingbirds |work=World Bird List Version 14.1 |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/hummingbirds/ |publisher=International Ornithologists' Union |access-date=2026-04-19}}</ref><ref name="clements">{{cite book |last=Clements |first=J. F. |display-authors=etal |title=The eBird/Clements checklist of Birds of the World: v2023 |year=2023 |url=https://www.birds.cornell.edu/clementschecklist/download/ |access-date=2026-04-19}}</ref>

* ''Heliodoxa rubinoides rubinoides'' <small>(Bourcier & Mulsant, 1846)</small> – Central and Eastern Andes of Colombia.
* ''Heliodoxa rubinoides aequatorialis'' <small>(Gould, 1860)</small> – Western slope of the Andes from western Colombia (Antioquia) south to western Ecuador (Pichincha to El Oro and western Loja).
* ''Heliodoxa rubinoides cervinigularis'' <small>(Salvin, 1892)</small> – Eastern slope of the Andes in eastern Ecuador and Peru; also recorded in northern Cochabamba, central Bolivia.

== Description ==
The fawn-breasted brilliant is a medium-sized hummingbird, measuring {{convert|11|to|13|cm|in|abbr=on}} in length and weighing approximately {{convert|7.2|to|10.2|g|oz|abbr=on}}.<ref name="AvianWebDesc">{{cite web |title=Fawn-breasted Brilliant Hummingbirds |url=https://earthlife.net/fawnbreastedbrillianthummingbirds/ |publisher=Earth Life |access-date=2026-04-19}}</ref><ref name="cervantes">{{cite web |title=Heliodoxa rubinoides |url=https://www.cervantesvirtual.com/obra/heliodoxa-rubinoides-857765/ |publisher=Biblioteca Virtual Miguel de Cervantes |access-date=2026-04-19 |language=es}}</ref> The bill is stout, fairly long ({{convert|22|–|27|mm|in|abbr=on}}), slightly decurved, and black.<ref name="ebird"/><ref name="Hilty277">{{cite book |last1=Hilty |first1=Steven L. |last2=Brown |first2=William L. |title=A Guide to the Birds of Colombia |year=1986 |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=0-691-08372-X |page=277}}</ref>

Adult males have shining bronzy-green upperparts, with coppery tones on the wing coverts and a slightly forked, coppery-green tail.<ref name="BOWAppearance">{{cite web |last1=Schulenberg |first1=T. S. |last2=Arendt |first2=D. |last3=Sedgwick |first3=C. W. |title=Fawn-breasted Brilliant (Heliodoxa rubinoides), version 1.0 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/fabbri1/cur/appearance |publisher=Birds of the World |year=2020 |access-date=2026-04-19}}</ref> The underparts are warm buffy-cinnamon, with irregular green spotting on the flanks and throat sides. A distinctive feature is the small, isolated pinkish to lilac [[gorget]] patch on the lower throat.<ref name="ebird"/> Females are similar but have duller plumage, more extensive green spotting on the underparts, and a smaller or absent throat patch.<ref name="ebird"/><ref name="BOWAppearance"/> Juveniles resemble females but have buffy fringes on the head feathers.<ref name="BOWAppearance"/>

== Distribution and habitat ==
The fawn-breasted brilliant is found in the [[Andes]] from western [[Colombia]] south to central [[Bolivia]].<ref name="IUCN"/> It inhabits humid [[montane forest]]s and [[cloud forest]]s, primarily in the lower to mid-levels of the forest interior.<ref name="BOWIntro">{{cite web |last1=Schulenberg |first1=T. S. |last2=Arendt |first2=D. |last3=Sedgwick |first3=C. W. |title=Fawn-breasted Brilliant (Heliodoxa rubinoides), version 1.0 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/fabbri1/cur/introduction |publisher=Birds of the World |year=2020 |access-date=2026-04-19}}</ref> It occasionally occurs at shaded forest borders, clearings, and near feeders.<ref name="ebird"/> The species is found at elevations between {{convert|1000|and|2600|m|ft|abbr=on}}, though most records are from {{convert|1100|to|2200|m|ft|abbr=on}}.<ref name="IUCN"/><ref name="ebird"/> It is generally uncommon and patchily distributed in natural habitats, but can be more frequently observed at nectar feeders.<ref name="ebird"/>

== Behavior ==
=== Diet ===
The [[diet (nutrition)|diet]] of the fawn-breasted brilliant consists primarily of [[nectar]] from a variety of brightly colored, tubular flowers. It supplements this with small [[insect]]s and [[spider]]s, which provide essential protein, especially during the breeding season.<ref name="avian"/> The bird catches insect prey by [[Hawking (birds)|hawking]] in flight, gleaning from foliage, and plucking from spider webs.<ref name="avian"/> When feeding on nectar, it hovers before the flower or perches and hangs from it, inserting its long tongue up to 13 times per second.<ref name="avian"/> The species is strongly territorial around high-quality nectar sources, aggressively chasing away intruders such as other hummingbirds, bumblebees, and hawk moths.<ref name="avian"/><ref name="Animalia">{{cite web |title=Fawn-breasted Brilliant |url=https://animalia.bio/fawn-breasted-brilliant |publisher=Animalia |access-date=2026-04-19}}</ref>

=== Breeding ===
The fawn-breasted brilliant is only social during the breeding season, which spans from August to December in much of its range.<ref name="BOWBreeding">{{cite web |last1=Schulenberg |first1=T. S. |last2=Arendt |first2=D. |last3=Sedgwick |first3=C. W. |title=Fawn-breasted Brilliant (Heliodoxa rubinoides), version 1.0 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/fabbri1/cur/breeding |publisher=Birds of the World |year=2020 |access-date=2026-04-19}}</ref> The male performs a courtship display by flying in U-shaped patterns before the female. There is no pair bond, and the male is not involved with nesting or the rearing of the young. Both males and females mate with several partners.<ref name="avian"/>

The female constructs a compact, cup-shaped nest in a [[shrub]] or tree, typically in the forest understory.<ref name="avian"/> The nest is made of plant fibers, spider webs, animal hairs, and down, and it is lined externally with moss for camouflage.<ref name="avian"/> She lays a clutch of two white eggs, which she incubates alone for approximately 18–20 days.<ref name="avian"/> The female broods the chicks for about 12 days, after which there is not enough room for her in the nest. She feeds the nestlings by regurgitating insects, as they cannot persist on nectar alone. The young fledge at about 20 days of age.<ref name="avian"/>

=== Vocalizations ===
{{birdsong|url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Heliodoxa-rubinoides |species=fawn-breasted brilliant}}
The fawn-breasted brilliant produces two main [[Animal communication|vocalizations]]: a series of sharp, metallic "tchik" notes, often given in rapid succession as an alarm or territorial warning, and a common "swi-swi-swi-swu" call.<ref name="BOWSounds">{{cite web |last1=Schulenberg |first1=T. S. |last2=Arendt |first2=D. |last3=Sedgwick |first3=C. W. |title=Fawn-breasted Brilliant (Heliodoxa rubinoides), version 1.0 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/fabbri1/cur/sounds |publisher=Birds of the World |year=2020 |access-date=2026-04-19}}</ref><ref name="XC">{{cite web |title=Heliodoxa rubinoides |url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Heliodoxa-rubinoides |publisher=Xeno-canto |access-date=2026-04-19}}</ref> Non-vocal communication includes the audible buzzing of wings during flight and displays, as well as tail fanning during aggressive or courtship interactions.<ref name="avian"/>

== Conservation ==
The fawn-breasted brilliant is classified as [[Least Concern]] on the [[IUCN Red List]] due to its extremely large geographic range, which exceeds {{convert|1,100,000|km2|sqmi|abbr=on}}.<ref name="IUCN"/> However, the global population trend is suspected to be decreasing as a result of ongoing habitat loss and fragmentation, primarily from deforestation, agricultural expansion, and mining.<ref name="IUCN"/><ref name="BirdLife">{{cite web |title=Fawn-breasted Brilliant Heliodoxa rubinoides |url=https://datazone.birdlife.org/species/factsheet/fawn-breasted-brilliant-heliodoxa-rubinoides |publisher=BirdLife International |access-date=2026-04-19}}</ref> The species is listed on Appendix II of [[CITES]], which regulates international trade.<ref name="CITES">{{cite web |title=Appendices |url=https://cites.org/eng/app/appendices.php |publisher=CITES |access-date=2026-04-19}}</ref> It occurs in several protected areas across its range, including [[Podocarpus National Park]] in Ecuador.<ref name="IUCN"/>

zh.extract:
棕胸辉蜂鸟（台湾作麂胸輝蜂鳥，学名：Heliodoxa rubinoides）是雨燕目蜂鸟科的一种鸟类。
棕胸辉蜂鸟体重约7.79克，翼长约69.3毫米，嘴峰长约27.7毫米，喙宽度约2.5毫米，喙厚度约2.3毫米，跗蹠长约5.2毫米，尾长约43.3毫米。棕胸辉蜂鸟在其分布区内为留鸟，其栖息在密集的高大树木组成的森林中，食性为草食性，主要食物来源是植物的花蜜。


亚种
H. r. rubinoides：分布于哥伦比亚的安第斯山脉中部和东部。
H. r. aequatorialis：分布于哥伦比亚的安第斯山脉西坡和厄瓜多尔西部。
H. r. cervinigularis：分布于厄瓜多尔东部和秘鲁东北部的安第斯山脉东坡。
